explanation: The phrase 'When enabled, the prop_0' suggests that whenever the 'enabled' condition is true, 'prop_0' must also be true. Since only 'prop_0' was given as an atomic proposition, I interpret this as 'globally, if prop_0 occurs, then enabled is true'. However, without an 'enabled' atomic proposition, this just expresses that when prop_0 is true, it is simply true. The natural mapping with only 'prop_0' is just to ensure when 'enabled' (i.e., prop_0) is true, then prop_0 is true, which is tautological. Therefore, the closest, given the constraints, is simply 'G(prop_0)'.

The phrase suggests that when some system is 'enabled', then prop_0 must be 'by' (likely meaning, is triggered or caused by) prop_1. In SAV notation, 'p by q' commonly means every rising occurrence of p is caused by a previous occurrence of q that hasn't been 'used up' already. In LTL, this is often specified with an implication: whenever prop_0 holds, previously prop_1 must have held since the last prop_0. But since the statement is vague and no explicit 'enabling' proposition is present, simply capturing this as 'if prop_0 holds, then previously prop_1 must have held' can be expressed as: G(prop_0 -> F prop_1), but more accurately, with causality: G(prop_0 -> (prop_1 | X(prop_1 | X(...)))), but this becomes just G(prop_0 -> F prop_1) under LTL. If 'enabled' implies the precondition always holds, then simply G(prop_0 -> F prop_1).

The phrase 'when prop_0, the prop_1 by the prop_2' means that whenever prop_0 holds, it implies that prop_1 will hold at least up to and including the next occurrence of prop_2. This pattern is typically encoded in LTL using implication and the 'release' operator, indicating that prop_1 holds until prop_2 occurs, whenever prop_0 holds.

Whenever prop_0 holds, then at some point in the future, prop_2 must become true before prop_1, and then prop_1 is activated. This is usually modeled such that if prop_0 becomes true, then eventually, prop_2 and then prop_1 occur (typically, prop_2 precedes prop_1). However, at a simple level, this can be encoded as: If prop_0 occurs, then eventually (in the future) both prop_2 and then prop_1 will occur, i.e., whenever prop_0 is true, then prop_2 must occur before or simultaneously with prop_1. In LTL, this can be expressed as: G (prop_0 -> ( ( !prop_1 ) U ( prop_2 & ( ( !prop_1 ) U prop_1 ) ) ) )

The phrase 'When enabled, the prop_0' suggests that whenever some 'enabled' condition holds, prop_0 should be true. However, since the only available atomic proposition is 'prop_0', and no 'enabled' signal is present, we can only formalize the requirement that 'prop_0' should always hold (globally true).

The phrase 'When prop_0, the prop_1 by the prop_2' is not fully grammatical. Interpreting it as: whenever prop_0 is true, then prop_1 holds at least until prop_2 holds. In other words: if prop_0 happens, then prop_1 holds at least up to (and possibly including) the point where prop_2 happens.
Whenever prop_0 holds, prop_2 will eventually hold, and after that, prop_1 must eventually hold.

This formalizes a requirement where if prop_0 holds at a state, then prop_1 must hold at some point after that, but before (or at the same time as) prop_2 holds. That is, after prop_0 becomes true, prop_1 must happen before (or unless) prop_2 happens.

The statement specifies that whenever 'prop_0' is true, then 'prop_1' must become true at some point and it is the responsibility of 'prop_2' to cause this. In strict LTL with only atomic propositions, we can specify the core requirement: whenever 'prop_0' holds, then 'prop_1' should eventually hold, and at the moment when 'prop_1' first holds after 'prop_0', 'prop_2' should also hold. This is modeled as: whenever 'prop_0' is true, eventually both 'prop_1' and 'prop_2' become true at the same moment.

The phrase 'when prop_0, the prop_1 by the prop_2' can be interpreted as: whenever prop_0 holds, then from that point, prop_1 holds at least until prop_2 holds. That is, prop_1 must persist as long as prop_0 holds and until or unless prop_2 becomes true.
